sources: docs/core/B2_KERNEL_HARDENING.md R7 Step-3 restatement + Blocked on decision: recovery-auth
docs/core/DECISIONS.md 50 recovery-auth (PROVISIONAL) — descriptor v2 BIP340 path; v1 parse-valid not invokable; b2h standing counter-design
docs/spec/WIRE_FORMAT.md §8.2 — proof normalized signingProfile equals descriptor's; a descriptor naming any other profile cannot be invoked

inputs.attackFlagText: This is the rare normative §8 sentence explicitly routed to B2 — losing it during kernel implementation would let a shape-valid future-profile descriptor be invoked under a bip322 proof.

fixture.caseUnsupportedProfileRejected: a descriptor armed with an unsupported profile (grammar-valid 'future-musig') OR a v1 descriptor (no recoveryPubkey) is invoked -> REJECT (never reaches pendingRecovery)
fixture.caseV2BIP340Passes: a v2 descriptor naming the BIP340 recovery-key path passes the profile gate -> the companion accept
fixture.priorState: an armed descriptor of the named profile/version

inputs.scopeNote: PROVISIONAL on recovery-auth (#50): under #50-b1 the supported invoke profile set is descriptor v2's BIP340 recovery-key path; v1 descriptors stay parse-valid but not invokable. The verdict (unsupported/future-profile or v1 descriptor never reaches pendingRecovery) is derivable under #50-b1 — the original encoded the pre-#50 wallet-proof profile gate. Normative §8.2 profile-equality anchors the 'unsupported profile not invokable' core, but #50 narrows §8.2's proof-equality leg to corroboration, so the kernel-acceptance shape depends on the provisional decision. #50 signer/evidence-shape axis (flip b1<->b2h); PR-34 owns the wider recovery transaction-shape/flags surface. Depends on #50 ⇒ provisional-vector.
inputs.authoringStatus: authored: provisional vector with decisionDeps + flipMarker; status proposed pending reviewer sign-off; flips on DK's #50 invoke-profile-gate ruling (b1 vs b2h).

expected.invariant: under #50-b1 only a descriptor v2 naming the supported BIP340 recovery-key path is invokable; a descriptor with an unsupported/future profile, or a v1 descriptor (no recoveryPubkey), is rejected and never reaches pendingRecovery (anchored by normative §8.2 profile-equality); identical on deterministic replay
expected.rationale: R7 is restated per recovery-auth (#50) and is marked 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth'. Under #50 (b1) the supported invoke profile set is descriptor v2's BIP340 recovery-key path; v1 descriptors stay parse-valid but not invokable. The verdict (unsupported/future-profile or v1 descriptor => never reaches pendingRecovery) is derivable, but only by adopting #50's provisional ruling — the original encoded the pre-#50 wallet-proof profile gate. The normative §8.2 profile-equality sentence still anchors the 'unsupported profile not invokable' core, but #50 narrows §8.2's proof-equality leg to corroboration, so the kernel-acceptance shape depends on the provisional decision.

flipMarker.flipsTo: rederive: if DK rules the b2h counter-design, the supported set re-admits the §8.2 proof<->descriptor profile-equality check as a kernel-acceptance input (rather than evidence-layer corroboration), and a v1 descriptor + wallet-proof remains invokable — flipping which descriptors pass the profile gate.

sources: docs/core/B2_KERNEL_HARDENING.md R9 Step-3 restatement + Blocked on decision: recovery-auth; b2h standing counter-design
docs/core/DECISIONS.md 50 — b1: BIP322 wallet proof non-authorizing corroboration vs b2h: BIP322/script/text verification inside the audited kernel
docs/core/SOFTWARE_CANON.md L2 row (ratified) — pure deterministic predicate, no network/clock

inputs.attackFlagText: BIP322 verification embeds Bitcoin script semantics — the B2 purity gate must treat the verifier as a deterministic pure function over (address, message, signature) with no network/UTXO lookups, or recovery verdicts stop being replayable.

fixture.caseValidProofInvalidEventSigRejected: a VALID wallet proof combined with an INVALID event BIP340 signature -> still REJECT (under #50-b1 the wallet proof is non-authorizing corroboration: its presence cannot substitute for the on-chain signature)
fixture.casePurity: identical (address, message, signature) yields a byte-identical BIP322 verdict with no I/O (the purity requirement holds under both b1 and b2h; ratified SOFTWARE_CANON L2)
fixture.priorState: a pending-recovery invoke carrying a valid wallet proof but a bad event signature

inputs.scopeNote: PROVISIONAL on recovery-auth (#50): the purity requirement (any BIP322 verification is a deterministic pure function over (address,message,signature), no network/UTXO) derives from ratified SOFTWARE_CANON and holds under either ruling. BUT under #50-b1 the BIP322 wallet proof is NON-authorizing corroboration (its absence/failure must not block; its presence must not substitute for the event BIP340 sig), whereas under the standing counter-design b2h the BIP322 verifier IS an authorizing kernel predicate. Whether BIP322 verification is even an acceptance-path predicate hinges on #50, so the verdict's shape is provisional. #50 signer/evidence-shape axis (flip b1<->b2h). Depends on #50 ⇒ provisional-vector.
inputs.authoringStatus: authored: provisional vector with decisionDeps + flipMarker; status proposed pending reviewer sign-off; flips on DK's #50 wallet-proof-authority ruling (b1 vs b2h).

expected.invariant: under #50-b1 the BIP322 wallet proof is non-authorizing corroboration: a valid wallet proof with an invalid event BIP340 signature is still rejected (corroboration cannot substitute for the on-chain signature); any BIP322 verification is a deterministic pure function over (address, message, signature) with no I/O (ratified SOFTWARE_CANON); identical on deterministic replay
expected.rationale: R9 is restated per recovery-auth (#50) and 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth'. The flag is the purity requirement: any BIP322 verification must be a deterministic pure function over (address, message, signature), no network/UTXO lookups, or recovery verdicts stop being replayable. The purity verdict itself derives from ratified SOFTWARE_CANON; BUT under #50 (b1) the BIP322 wallet proof is non-authorizing corroboration (its absence/failure must not block, presence must not substitute), whereas under the standing counter-design b2h the BIP322 verifier IS inside the audited kernel as an authorizing input. Whether BIP322 verification is even an acceptance-path predicate hinges on #50, so the vector's expected shape is provisional.

flipMarker.flipsTo: rederive: if DK rules the b2h counter-design, the BIP322 verifier becomes an authorizing kernel predicate (descriptor + wallet-proof evidence + BIP322/script/text verification inside the audited kernel), so absence or failure of the proof DOES block acceptance — flipping this vector from reject-on-bad-event-sig (proof non-authorizing) to a model where the proof authorizes; the purity-of-the-verifier requirement persists under both.

sources: docs/core/B2_KERNEL_HARDENING.md R10 Step-3 restatement — drafted against b1 with ratification flip marker; Blocked on decision: recovery-auth
docs/core/DECISIONS.md 50 — fresh BIP340 over W13 digest; Replay: digest binds prevStateTxid, W13 domain separation; negative test replayed-arming-sig-as-invoke
docs/spec/ONT_RECOVERY_INVOKE_SPEC.md What's missing item 2 — invoke-path signer is the open protocol question

inputs.attackFlagText: Option (a) would make the slot a static, publicly-known value (the arming signature) replayable by anyone who has seen the descriptor — the ruling must address replay; flagged, not decided.
inputs.predicateSignature: recoverInvokeSig(invoke{64-byte signatureField}, armedDescriptor{recoveryPubkey}, prevStateTxid, W13digest, priorState) -> {verdict}

fixture.caseReplayedArmingValueRejected: a replayed / static arming-style value (option-a slot content) presented in the event's signature field -> REJECT (it fails the W13-digest fresh-BIP340 check; the digest binds prevStateTxid so a captured signature dies when the state head moves, and W13 domain separation blocks cross-context reuse)
fixture.caseFreshBIP340Accepts: a fresh BIP340 signature by recoveryPubkey over the W13 digest -> the companion accept
fixture.priorState: an armed descriptor; an attacker has seen the descriptor and replays its arming-style value as an invoke

inputs.scopeNote: PROVISIONAL on recovery-auth (#50): under #50-b1 the 64-byte field carries a fresh BIP340 signature over the W13 digest (which binds prevStateTxid -> a captured sig dies when the state head moves -> plus W13 domain separation), so a replayed static arming value is rejected. Option-(a) acceptance vs (b1) rejection is exactly what #50 decides; the hardening doc drafts this against b1 with a ratification flip marker. #50 signer-shape axis (flip b1<->b2h). Depends on #50 ⇒ provisional-vector.
inputs.authoringStatus: authored: provisional vector with decisionDeps + flipMarker; status proposed pending reviewer sign-off; flips on DK's #50 invoke-slot ruling (b1 vs b2h).

expected.invariant: under #50-b1 the invoke's 64-byte field is a fresh BIP340 signature by recoveryPubkey over the W13 digest; a replayed/static arming-style value fails that check and is rejected (the digest binds prevStateTxid and W13 domain separation kill replay); a genuine fresh BIP340 is accepted; identical on deterministic replay
expected.rationale: R10 is restated per recovery-auth (#50, provisional), 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth', and explicitly says vectors are drafted against b1 with a flip marker should DK rule b2h. This flag is the replay concern: option (a) (arming-signature-in-slot) is a static replayable value; the ruling must address replay. #50 (b1) resolves it: the 64-byte field carries a fresh BIP340 sig over the W13 digest which binds prevStateTxid (captured sig dies when state head moves) + W13 domain separation. So 'replayed arming sig presented as invoke => reject' is derivable under #50. Provisional because option-(a) acceptance vs (b1) rejection is exactly what #50 decides.

flipMarker.flipsTo: rederive: if DK rules the b2h counter-design, the authorizing artifact is the BIP322 wallet-proof commitment, so the replay analysis and the accepted slot content change accordingly (the fresh-BIP340-over-W13-digest check is replaced by the b2h custody-proof check).

sources: docs/core/B2_KERNEL_HARDENING.md R10 Needed spec work — reconcile §4.2 signature field with legacy commitment-in-slot; Blocked on recovery-auth
docs/core/DECISIONS.md 50 — 64-byte field carries fresh BIP340 over W13 digest; ratified §8.3 proof commitment is 32 bytes

inputs.attackFlagText: Normative wire text labels the field `signature`(64, Schnorr) while the only deployed behavior put a non-signature commitment there — divergence between normative naming and legacy evidence.

fixture.caseLegacyCommitmentInSlotRejected: an invoke whose 64-byte field contains the LEGACY non-signature commitment (proofHash || 32 reserved zero bytes) rather than a BIP340 signature -> REJECT (not a valid signature over the W13 digest); under #50-b1 the normative §4.2 'signature' naming wins and the legacy commitment-in-slot is rejected
fixture.fitNote: the ratified §8.3 proof commitment is now 32 bytes, so the legacy 64-byte commitment packing no longer fits the field
fixture.priorState: an armed descriptor; an invoke carrying the legacy commitment-in-slot rather than a signature

inputs.scopeNote: PROVISIONAL on recovery-auth (#50): divergence between normative §4.2 naming (signature, 64, Schnorr) and the only deployed behavior (legacy filled the slot with proofHash || 32 reserved zero bytes — a commitment, not a signature). Under #50-b1 the field's meaning is a fresh BIP340 signature over the W13 digest (the normative naming wins; the legacy commitment-in-slot is rejected; the ratified §8.3 proof commitment is now 32 bytes so the legacy 64-byte packing no longer fits). Derivable under #50; under b2h the slot semantics differ. #50 signer-shape axis (flip b1<->b2h). Depends on #50 ⇒ provisional-vector.
inputs.authoringStatus: authored: provisional vector with decisionDeps + flipMarker; status proposed pending reviewer sign-off; flips on DK's #50 invoke-slot-semantics ruling (b1 vs b2h).

expected.invariant: under #50-b1 the RecoverOwner 64-byte field is a BIP340 signature (per normative §4.2 naming); an invoke whose field carries the legacy non-signature commitment (proofHash || 32 reserved zero bytes) is not a valid signature over the W13 digest and is rejected; identical on deterministic replay
expected.rationale: Second R10 flag: divergence between normative §4.2 naming (signature, 64, Schnorr) and the only deployed behavior (legacy filled the slot with proofHash || 32 reserved zero bytes — a commitment, not a signature). Under #50 (b1) the field's meaning is ratified provisionally as a fresh BIP340 signature over the W13 digest — i.e. the normative naming wins and the legacy commitment-in-slot is rejected. The verdict is derivable under #50 (which explicitly notes the ratified §8.3 proof commitment is now 32 bytes so the legacy 64-byte commitment packing no longer fits). Provisional: under b2h the slot semantics differ, so the accepted content flips.

flipMarker.flipsTo: rederive: if DK rules the b2h counter-design, the field/commitment semantics are redefined per the cold-hardware design, changing what occupies and validates the 64-byte slot (the BIP340-signature reading is replaced by the b2h commitment semantics).
